skill.name: documentation-audit
skill.description: Verifies documentation claims against the current codebase using claim extraction, evidence collection, and pattern expansion.
skill.summary: Audit documentation for drift, stale references, wrong defaults, and unsupported claims.
skill.author: 2389-research

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command, environment, and weak-crypto patterns, but most are Markdown examples, regex snippets, or documentation audit checklists rather than executable skill code. No prompt injection, network exfiltration, or malicious intent was found. The main confirmed risk is that the workflow can inspect repository files and may run local project commands, including a sample Python --help invocation that could execute untrusted code.

medium_findings.title: Local Project Command Execution Guidance
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill includes legitimate documentation-audit commands, but the CLI verification example runs a project script with Python. Running a repository script, even with --help, can execute untrusted code during an audit.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command is explicitly shown as a verification step. It appears intended for legitimate flag validation, but executing project scripts is a real local code execution risk.

medium_findings.title: Broad Repository Inspection Workflow
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The workflow directs the agent to search documentation and code, list scripts, check file existence, and compare configuration references. This is expected for the skill, but it requires filesystem access and can reveal local project structure and configuration names.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Multiple checklist steps require reading files and verifying repository artifacts. The behavior is central to the skill, so it is a managed operational risk rather than malicious activity.

low_findings.title: Static Analyzer False Positives in Markdown Examples
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: Many external command findings are from backticked terms, shell examples, regex snippets, and sample output formats in Markdown. They are not hidden executable code and do not show exfiltration or persistence behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged content is visibly inside Markdown instructions or fenced examples. No executable skill script or automation wrapper is present in the scanned files.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Pattern False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The weak-crypto alerts appear to match ordinary documentation text rather than cryptographic API use. No MD5, SHA1, DES, or encryption implementation evidence was found in the scanned skill files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The relevant lines contain documentation prose such as descriptions, design docs, or filter categories. There is no cryptographic code or instruction to use weak algorithms.

low_findings.title: Environment Reference Examples Without Secret Exfiltration
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The environment findings refer to checking .env.example files, documented variable names, and os.getenv examples. No evidence found that the skill reads real secrets or sends environment values outside the workspace.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The content discusses documented configuration verification and sample getenv patterns. The scanned files contain no network destination or credential collection logic.

dangerous_patterns.title: Execute Project Script For CLI Help
dangerous_patterns.description: The sample command runs python scripts/foo.py --help during documentation verification. Auditors should prefer static argparse inspection before executing untrusted repository scripts.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command is explicit and can execute module top-level code. The surrounding context is legitimate verification guidance, so the risk is moderate.

content.value_statement: Documentation often drifts after releases, refactors, and configuration changes. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code verify claims against repository evidence and produce an actionable audit report.
actual_capabilities: Extracts verifiable claims from user-facing documentation files.
Classifies claims such as file references, defaults, environment variables, CLI flags, and behavior.
Checks claims against repository files, configuration examples, code defaults, and script interfaces.
Expands from false claims to find related stale references across documentation.
Produces a documentation audit report with evidence, fixes, metrics, and human review items.
Separates auto-verifiable claims from behavior claims that need human judgment.
limitations: Behavioral claims may need human review when code evidence is indirect.
The workflow can require broad read access to documentation and source files.
Some command examples should be reviewed before execution in untrusted repositories.
It does not automatically rewrite documentation unless the user requests fixes.

use_cases.title: Pre-release Documentation QA
use_cases.description: Verify README and docs before a release so stale paths, flags, and defaults are caught early.

use_cases.title: Refactor Drift Review
use_cases.description: Find documentation claims that no longer match renamed files, deleted scripts, or changed configuration.

use_cases.title: Technical Writing Evidence Check
use_cases.description: Create an evidence-backed queue of documentation claims that need updates or human review.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Main Documentation
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of common user-facing docs.
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit the README and docs folder for claims that do not match the current codebase. Produce a concise report with evidence and suggested fixes.

prompt_templates.title: Check Configuration Claims
prompt_templates.scenario: Focused review of defaults and environment variables.
prompt_templates.prompt: Verify all documented configuration defaults and environment variables against .env.example and the source code. Mark each claim true, false, or needs review.

prompt_templates.title: Find Drift After Refactor
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate audit after files, scripts, or services changed.
prompt_templates.prompt: Run a documentation drift audit focused on file references, script names, CLI flags, and service names changed by the recent refactor.

prompt_templates.title: Run Full Two-pass Audit
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced audit with pattern expansion and gap detection.
prompt_templates.prompt: Perform the full two-pass documentation audit. Extract claims, verify evidence, expand false-claim patterns, compare documented items to code inventory, and write the audit report.

output_examples.input: Audit README.md before the next release.
output: Executive summary with documents scanned and claim counts.
False claims grouped by document with evidence and suggested fixes.
Human review queue for behavior claims that need confirmation.

output_examples.input: Check whether documented environment variables are still accurate.
output: List of documented variables that exist in examples and code.
List of missing or deprecated variables with file references.
Recommended documentation updates for changed defaults.

output_examples.input: Review docs after scripts were renamed.
output: Dead script references found across docs.
Related stale command examples discovered by pattern expansion.
Gap report comparing documented scripts to actual script files.

best_practices: Start with user-facing documentation and exclude plans, audits, and historical design notes.
Record file and line evidence for every true, false, or needs-review verdict.
Inspect command examples before running them in untrusted repositories.
anti_patterns: Do not rely on visual review when a claim can be verified against code.
Do not skip pattern expansion after finding repeated stale claims.
Do not execute project scripts during verification when static inspection is enough.

faq.question: What documentation should I audit first?
faq.answer: Start with README files, user guides, configuration docs, and release-facing documentation.

faq.question: Does this skill edit documentation automatically?
faq.answer: No. It creates an evidence-backed audit report unless you separately ask for documentation fixes.

faq.question: Can it verify behavior claims?
faq.answer: It can collect supporting evidence, but indirect behavior claims are usually marked for human review.

faq.question: Why does it use two passes?
faq.answer: The second pass searches for related issues after one false claim reveals a drift pattern.

faq.question: Is it safe for private repositories?
faq.answer: It works locally, but it needs read access to relevant docs and source files to verify claims.

faq.question: What tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
